title: New business at the Lakeside Association
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the monthly meeting of the Lakeside Community Association. The chair turns to new business.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We'll now move to new business.

turns.speaker: Member (Ramos)
turns.line: I move that the Association spend $500 on new signage.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Before I do anything, what needs to happen with this motion?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what must happen before this main motion is debated?
turns.gold: A main motion requires a second before it is considered. The chair should call for a second; if seconded, the chair states the question and opens it to debate.

ref.quote: The basic requirement for approval of an action or choice by a deliberative assembly, except where a rule provides otherwise, is a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: It is moved and seconded to spend $500 on new signage. Is there any debate?

turns.speaker: Member (Park)
turns.line: I move to amend by striking out '$500' and inserting '$750'.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is this amendment handled, and what vote does it need?
turns.gold: The amendment needs a second and must be germane to the motion. It is debatable and is adopted by a majority vote — even though the main motion might require a different threshold, an amendment needs only a majority.

ref.quote: A secondary motion thus takes precedence over the main motion; and a main motion takes precedence over nothing and yields to all applicable secondary motions.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second the amendment!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The amendment carries. The motion now reads 'spend $750 on new signage.' Further debate?

turns.speaker: Member (Ramos)
turns.line: We've heard enough — I move the previous question!
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is the motion for the Previous Question handled?
turns.gold: The Previous Question needs a second, is not debatable, and requires a two-thirds vote to adopt. If it passes, debate ends immediately and the assembly votes on the pending motion.

ref.quote: The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The previous question is adopted by more than two-thirds; debate is closed. (to you) What vote is now needed to adopt the main motion itself?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what vote adopts this main motion?
turns.gold: A main motion is adopted by a majority vote unless a special rule or the bylaws require more. Here a majority of the votes cast decides it.

ref.quote: The basic requirement for approval of an action or choice by a deliberative assembly, except where a rule provides otherwise, is a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The motion is adopted. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: A thin house at the Booster Club
setting: The Booster Club meeting is scheduled to start, but only a few members are present. A member is eager to get going.

turns.speaker: Member (Doyle)
turns.line: There aren't many of us, but let's just start adopting motions.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what must be confirmed before the assembly transacts business?
turns.gold: Business cannot be transacted without a quorum present. The chair must confirm that a quorum, as defined by the bylaws, is present before the assembly takes any substantive action.

ref.quote: A quorum in an assembly is the number of members … who must be present in order that business can be validly transacted.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Thank you. Members have arrived and we now have a quorum. Proceed.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: Point of order! I don't think the last speaker was recognized.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is a Point of Order handled?
turns.gold: A Point of Order needs no second, is not debatable, and is raised at the time of the breach. The chair rules on the point; the chair's ruling may be challenged by an Appeal.

ref.quote: When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or "raise a question of order," as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) I'm inclined to rule the point well taken — but should I make this ruling, or should you as the parliamentarian?
turns.prompt: Advise: who rules on the point — you or the chair?
turns.gold: The chair rules, not the parliamentarian. The parliamentarian only advises the chair (usually privately); the chair then states the ruling to the assembly.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian is a consultant, commonly a professional, who advises the president and other officers, committees, and members on matters of parliamentary procedure.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Understood. I rule the point well taken.

title: A close election at the Arts Guild
setting: The Arts Guild is electing its president. The bylaws require a ballot vote but are silent on the threshold. Three candidates are running.

turns.speaker: Teller
turns.line: First ballot: Alvarez 40, Brooks 35, Cho 20. Alvarez has the most votes.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Alvarez has a plurality. Can I declare Alvarez elected?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: is a plurality enough to elect here?
turns.gold: No. Unless the bylaws provide otherwise, election requires a majority — more than half of the votes cast. A plurality does not elect.

ref.quote: A plurality vote is the largest number of votes to be given any candidate or proposition when three or more choices are possible; the candidate or proposition receiving the largest number of votes has a plurality.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We balloted again: Alvarez 48, Brooks 42, Cho 5 — still no one over half. (to you) What do we do now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what happens when no candidate has a majority?
turns.gold: Balloting is simply repeated until one candidate receives a majority. Candidates are not dropped unless the bylaws so provide, and members may change their votes on each ballot.

ref.quote: A plurality vote is the largest number of votes to be given any candidate or proposition when three or more choices are possible; the candidate or proposition receiving the largest number of votes has a plurality.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Third ballot: Alvarez has 51 of 95 votes — a majority. Alvarez is elected.

title: A dues increase at the Historical Society
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Historical Society. A bylaw amendment to raise annual dues from $50 to $75 was submitted in writing at last month's regular meeting, and is now up for consideration.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Next: the bylaw amendment to raise dues from $50 to $75, noticed last month.

turns.speaker: Member (Vance)
turns.line: Let's just pass it with a simple majority and move on.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what vote does adopting this bylaw amendment require?
turns.gold: Amending the bylaws takes a two-thirds vote, and it is in order now only because previous notice was given at the last regular meeting. A simple majority is not sufficient.

ref.quote: These bylaws may be amended at any regular meeting of the Society by a two-thirds vote, provided that the amendment has been submitted in writing at the previous regular meeting.

turns.speaker: Member (Ruiz)
turns.line: I move to amend the amendment — make the new dues $150 instead of $75.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: is amending the figure up to $150 in order?
turns.gold: No. An amendment to a bylaw amendment cannot exceed the scope of the notice. Members were noticed of an increase to $75, so a higher figure like $150 is out of order; a figure between the current $50 and the noticed $75 would be in order.

ref.quote: If the bylaws require previous notice for their amendment … no amendment to a bylaw amendment is in order that increases the modification of the article or provision to be amended.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The amendment to $150 is out of order — it exceeds the scope of the notice. We'll vote on the $75 amendment as noticed.

title: Reworking a proposal at the Riverside Garden Club
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Riverside Garden Club. A main motion "that the Club hold its annual plant sale on the first Saturday of May" is pending and has been debated for a while.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The motion to hold the plant sale on the first Saturday of May is open for debate.

turns.speaker: Member (Okafor)
turns.line: This needs more study than we can give it on the floor. I move to refer the motion to a three-member committee to work out the details.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is this motion to Commit/Refer handled, and what vote does it need?
turns.gold: The motion to Commit or Refer needs a second and is debatable. It sends the pending question to a committee and is adopted by a majority vote. If it passes, the main motion goes to the committee instead of being decided now.

ref.quote: The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The motion to refer is defeated, so the main motion is again pending. Further debate?

turns.speaker: Member (Bianchi)
turns.line: I move to substitute the whole motion with "that the Club hold a fall harvest festival in October instead of a plant sale."
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: is a substitute in order here, and how is it treated?
turns.gold: A motion to substitute is a form of amendment and must be germane to the pending question. It needs a second, is fully debatable on the merits of both the original and the substitute, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: A primary amendment to substitute is treated similarly to a motion to strike out and insert … It is open to debate at all times while it is pending with no secondary amendment pending; and such debate may go fully into the merits of both the original text and the substitute.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The substitute is lost; the original motion stands. It's getting late.

turns.speaker: Member (Okafor)
turns.line: I move to postpone this motion to our next regular meeting.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time handled?
turns.gold: The motion to Postpone to a Certain Time needs a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote. It puts the question off to a definite later time — here the next regular meeting. (A two-thirds vote is needed only if it is made a special order.)

ref.quote: The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The motion is postponed to our next regular meeting. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: Undoing a hasty vote at the Ridgeline Trail Alliance
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Ridgeline Trail Alliance. Earlier in this same meeting the assembly adopted a motion to spend $2,000 on a trailhead sign. New information has just come to light.

turns.speaker: Member (Nguyen)
turns.line: I move to spend an additional $800 on landscaping around the new sign.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: That motion is out of order because we already adjourned that item.

turns.speaker: Member (Nguyen)
turns.line: I disagree with that ruling and I appeal from the decision of the chair!
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is this Appeal handled, and who decides it?
turns.gold: An Appeal requires a second. It takes the question away from the chair and gives it to the assembly, which decides by vote. A majority or a tie vote sustains the chair's ruling; the assembly, not the chair, has the final say.

ref.quote: But any two members have the right to Appeal from his decision on such a question. By one member making (or "taking") the appeal and another seconding it, the question is taken from the chair and vested in the assembly for final decision.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: On the appeal, the assembly reverses my ruling. (to you) Now a member wants to revisit the $2,000 sign vote we adopted earlier tonight.

turns.speaker: Member (Alvarado)
turns.line: I voted for the $2,000, but the bids came in higher. I move to reconsider that vote.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: may this member move to Reconsider the vote?
turns.gold: Yes. In an ordinary meeting the motion to Reconsider must be made the same day as the vote, and only by a member who voted on the prevailing side. Alvarado voted for the motion that was adopted, so he is on the prevailing side and may move it. It needs a second.

ref.quote: The motion to Reconsider can be made only by a member who voted with the prevailing side. In other words, a reconsideration can be moved only by one who voted aye if the motion involved was adopted, or no if the motion was lost.

turns.speaker: Member (Alvarado)
turns.line: Actually, rather than reconsider, could we just change the amount that's already on the books to $2,500?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what motion is used to change a figure the assembly already adopted?
turns.gold: That is the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted, used to change part of an action already taken. Without previous notice it takes a two-thirds vote or a majority of the entire membership; with notice, a majority vote.

ref.quote: Amend Something Previously Adopted is the motion that can be used if it is desired to change only a part of the text, or to substitute a different version.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Understood. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: The parliamentarian's proper role at the Metro Nonprofit Convention
setting: You have been engaged as the professional parliamentarian for the annual convention of the Metro Nonprofit Coalition. A contentious bylaws item is on the floor and tempers are rising.

turns.speaker: Member (Delacroix)
turns.line: (to you) You clearly know the rules best — just tell us how to vote and rule on this point for us.
turns.prompt: Advise: what is your proper role, and who actually rules?
turns.gold: The parliamentarian's role during a meeting is purely advisory and consultative. Parliamentary law gives the chair alone the power to rule on questions of order; I advise the chair — usually privately — and the chair makes and states the ruling.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian is a consultant, commonly a professional, who advises the president and other officers, committees, and members on matters of parliamentary procedure.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) You're also a dues-paying member of this Coalition. Can you just vote with us on this bylaws amendment to help it pass?
turns.prompt: Advise: as the member serving as parliamentarian, may you vote and debate on this question?
turns.gold: No. A member who serves as parliamentarian has the same duty as the chair to maintain impartiality, and so does not make motions, debate, or vote on questions — except on a ballot vote. I must forgo those rights while serving in this role.

ref.quote: A member of an assembly who acts as its parliamentarian has the same duty as the presiding officer to maintain a position of impartiality, and therefore does not make motions, participate in debate, or vote on any question except in the case of a ballot vote.

turns.speaker: Member (Delacroix)
turns.line: This amendment is a fraud and the member who wrote it is a liar!
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how should the chair handle this remark in debate?
turns.gold: Debate must be directed to the measure, not the member. A member may not attack another's motives or use words like 'fraud' or 'liar' about a member. The chair must act at once to call the speaker to order and require decorum.

ref.quote: When a question is pending, a member can condemn the nature or likely consequences of the proposed measure in strong terms, but he must avoid personalities, and under no circumstances can he attack or question the motives of another member. The measure, not the member, is the subject of debate.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The member will confine his remarks to the measure. Thank you, parliamentarian.
